race.name: Gran Fondo Jasper
race.tagline: Canada's ONLY fondo inside a national park. 165 km through the Canadian Rockies. Marmot Basin — Alberta's #1 categorized climb. The Icefields Parkway. Jasper National Park, UNESCO World Heritage Site. Running since 2014.

vitals.registration: Online via Race Roster. Charity fundraising entry at $115 CAD (requires $750 fundraising commitment). Standard registration pricing in CAD, closes at noon MST the day before. All prices subject to 5% GST.
vitals.entry_fee: ~$115-175 CAD depending on distance and registration timing
vitals.feed_zones: Fully stocked aid stations along routes. On-course support. National park roads provide wide shoulders and relative traffic safety on event day.
vitals.cutoff_time: Course support has time limits. Riders must maintain reasonable pace for their chosen distance.
route_options: Forte Fondo: 190 km, 2,339 m climbing — Marmot Basin + Edith Cavell Road + Icefields Parkway to Poboktan Creek. Capped at 75 riders.
Gran Fondo: 165 km, 1,691 m climbing — Marmot Basin climb + Icefields Parkway south to Poboktan Creek turnaround
Leg Burner Fondo: 126 km, 1,899 m climbing — Forte route to Athabasca Falls, then south on 93A/Icefields Parkway to Goatlick turnaround
Medio Fondo: 100 km, 1,038 m climbing — Marmot Basin climb + rolling terrain along Hwy 93A
Piccolo Fondo: 65 km, 601 m climbing — Highway 93A loop, no major climbs, scenic Jasper townsite loop
Hill Climb: 65 km — Dedicated Marmot Basin hill climb category

climate.primary: Canadian Rockies alpine — cool mornings, mild afternoons, June rainfall
climate.description: Early June in the Canadian Rockies delivers long days and cool temperatures, but Jasper's June is also its wettest month. Expect average highs around 18-21°C (64-70°F) and lows dropping to 4-9°C (39-48°F) — mornings at the start line will be cold. With 17 hours of daylight (sunrise at 5:05 AM, sunset at 10:02 PM), there is ample time to complete even the 190 km Forte distance. However, rain is expected on roughly 14-17 days in June, with approximately 117 mm of rainfall for the month. The Icefields Parkway corridor, sitting at higher elevations south of Jasper, can be significantly colder than town, with headwinds common on the return north. Mountain weather changes rapidly — sunshine at the start does not guarantee dry conditions at Poboktan Creek 80 km south.
challenges: June is Jasper's wettest month — expect rain on roughly half the days, with sudden mountain storms possible
Morning temperatures at 4-9°C demand arm warmers, vest, and potentially a rain jacket at the start
Icefields Parkway corridor creates wind tunnel effects — headwinds on the return leg are common
Elevation difference between Jasper townsite (1,060 m) and Marmot Basin (~1,830 m) creates 10-15°C temperature swings
Post-2024 wildfire landscape may affect microclimate conditions with reduced tree canopy in burn zones

terrain.primary: Paved mountain highway through Jasper National Park
terrain.surface: All paved roads within Jasper National Park. The Icefields Parkway (Highway 93) features wide shoulders and well-maintained pavement — one of the world's great scenic driving roads. Marmot Basin Road is a quiet mountain road climbing to the ski resort, ranked Alberta's #1 categorized climb. Edith Cavell Road (Forte route only) is narrower and winding with tight switchbacks. Highway 93A provides a quieter parallel route to the Icefields Parkway. Note: the final 1.5 km of the Marmot Basin climb may include a short dirt section, but it is easily rideable on a road bike.
features: Marmot Basin Climb — 13-14 km at 5.4-5.8% average gradient, 770-828 m gain, Alberta's #1 categorized climb per Canadian Cycling Magazine
Icefields Parkway — one of the world's great scenic corridors, glaciers and mountain peaks visible throughout
Mount Edith Cavell Road (Forte only) — 19 km at 3.4% average with tight switchbacks, narrow shoulders, scenic to the wilderness hostel
Athabasca Falls — landmark waypoint on the Forte and Leg Burner routes, 30 km south of Jasper
Poboktan Creek turnaround (Gran/Forte) — the deepest point into the Icefields Parkway at ~1,540 m elevation

course_description.character: Every route option starts and finishes in Jasper townsite, radiating outward through the Canadian Rockies like spokes from a hub. The signature Gran Fondo (165 km) heads west on Connaught Drive, picks up the Icefields Parkway, then turns onto Highway 93A before attacking Marmot Basin — a 13-14 km climb averaging 5.4-5.8% to the ski resort base lodge, ranked Alberta's #1 categorized climb. After descending Marmot, the route continues south on the Icefields Parkway toward Poboktan Creek, where glaciers and 3,000 m peaks frame the turnaround before riders retrace north to Jasper. The Forte Fondo (190 km) adds Edith Cavell Road — 19 km of narrow, winding switchbacks climbing to the Mount Edith Cavell Wilderness Hostel with views of Angel Glacier — between Marmot and the Icefields Parkway push. The Leg Burner (126 km) reaches Athabasca Falls before routing south to Goatlick on the Parkway. The Medio (100 km) includes Marmot Basin and rolling 93A terrain. The Piccolo (65 km) loops through 93A without major climbs. Throughout every distance, you are inside a UNESCO World Heritage national park surrounded by the Canadian Rockies — elk on the roadside, glaciers on the horizon, and mountain air that tastes different from anywhere else.
course_description.signature_challenge: Marmot Basin Climb. Alberta's #1 categorized road climb per Canadian Cycling Magazine. 13-14 km at 5.4-5.8% average gradient with 770-828 m of elevation gain. Maximum gradient 9.3%. Featured as Stage 4 of the 2015 Tour of Alberta, won by WorldTour rider Tom-Jelte Slagter. The climb winds through subalpine forest to the Marmot Basin ski resort base lodge, with panoramic views of the Athabasca Valley from the upper reaches. Steady, scenic, and relentless — the climb that defines the event.
suffering_zones: Icefields Parkway South to Poboktan Creek

logistics.airport: Edmonton International Airport (YEG) — 3.5-4 hour drive west via Highway 16. The only realistic major airport. Calgary (YYC) is 4.5+ hours via the Icefields Parkway or Highway 11. VIA Rail connects Edmonton and Vancouver to Jasper but schedules are infrequent.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Book early — Jasper is a small national park town (~4,800 residents) with limited lodging that books months ahead for June. The 2024 wildfire destroyed 358 properties, further constraining supply. Options include Fairmont Jasper Park Lodge (luxury, $400+ CAD/night), mid-range hotels on Connaught Drive ($200-350 CAD), hostels (HI Jasper, $40-80 CAD), and Parks Canada campgrounds ($25-40 CAD, reserve at reservation.pc.gc.ca). Hinton, Alberta (45 min east on Hwy 16) offers significantly cheaper lodging as a budget alternative. Airbnb supply in Jasper is minimal post-wildfire.
logistics.food: Jasper townsite has a range of restaurants from casual to upscale — Fiddle River (seafood), Evil Dave's Grill, and The Raven Bistro are local favorites. Bear's Paw Bakery is the essential pre-ride breakfast stop. Grocery options include Robinson's IGA and a small Safeway — national park pricing applies. Post-ride options cluster on Patricia Street and Connaught Drive. For the ride itself, pack nutrition aggressively — the Icefields Parkway has no services between aid stations.

history.origin_story: Gran Fondo Jasper launched in 2014 as Canada's first and only gran fondo held entirely within a national park. The event was born from a simple recognition: Jasper National Park contains some of the most spectacular road cycling terrain in North America — the Icefields Parkway, Marmot Basin Road, and the mountain highways of the Canadian Rockies — yet no organized event existed to showcase it. The Marmot Basin climb had already earned recognition as Alberta's top categorized road climb from Canadian Cycling Magazine. When the 2015 Tour of Alberta brought WorldTour professionals to race up Marmot Basin as Stage 4 (won by Tom-Jelte Slagter of Cannondale-Garmin), the climb's pedigree was confirmed at the highest level. The event grew steadily through the 2010s, establishing partnerships with MNP as title sponsor and the Jasper Yellowhead Historical Society as charity partner. The July 2024 wildfire that destroyed 30% of Jasper's structures — 358 properties and an estimated $1.2 billion in insured damage — tested the event's resilience. The 2025 edition proceeded with potential course modifications, and the 2026 10th anniversary edition represents a statement of community recovery.
notable_moments: 2014: Inaugural Gran Fondo Jasper — Canada's first fondo inside a national park
2015: Marmot Basin featured as Stage 4 of the Tour of Alberta, won by WorldTour rider Tom-Jelte Slagter (Cannondale-Garmin)
2024: 10th running draws 300+ riders; weeks later, the July 2024 Jasper wildfire destroys 358 properties and 30% of the town's structures
2025: Event continues post-wildfire as part of Jasper's recovery narrative, with potential course adjustments for fire mitigation
2026: 10th anniversary edition (by event numbering) scheduled for June 6 — six distances from 65-190 km, the Forte Fondo capped at 75 riders
history.reputation: The definitive national park cycling experience in Canada. Known for world-class scenery rather than competitive depth. The Marmot Basin climb carries WorldTour credibility from the 2015 Tour of Alberta. The post-wildfire editions carry emotional weight as part of Jasper's community recovery.

biased_opinion.summary: Gran Fondo Jasper exists because no other organized cycling event in North America can put you inside a national park for 165 km of mountain riding. That is the entire value proposition, and it is enormous. The Icefields Parkway is not merely a beautiful road — it is consistently ranked among the most spectacular drives on Earth, and you get to ride it on a bicycle surrounded by glaciers, turquoise rivers, and peaks that pierce 3,000 meters. Marmot Basin — Alberta's top categorized climb, tested by WorldTour riders during the 2015 Tour of Alberta — delivers legitimate suffering on the way to the treeline. The Forte Fondo adds Edith Cavell Road for 190 km and 2,339 m of climbing through one of the Rockies' most iconic mountain approaches. This is not a race. It is 300 people riding through a cathedral of mountains in a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The 2024 wildfire that destroyed a third of Jasper adds a raw dimension — you ride through a landscape that is simultaneously spectacular and scarred, through a community that is rebuilding. The logistics are the price of admission: remote, expensive to reach, weather-dependent. But if you ride a road bike and want a single day of riding that you will remember for the rest of your life, this is it.
strengths: Entirely within Jasper National Park — UNESCO World Heritage Site, Canadian Rockies
Icefields Parkway — one of the world's most spectacular roads, glaciers and peaks throughout
Marmot Basin — Alberta's #1 categorized climb, 2015 Tour of Alberta stage, 13-14 km at 5.4-5.8%
Six distance options from 65-190 km accommodate all fitness levels
10th anniversary edition in 2026 — decade of organizational refinement in a national park setting
weaknesses: Remote location — Edmonton YEG is 3.5-4 hours away, no closer major airport
June is Jasper's wettest month — expect rain on roughly half the days
Post-2024 wildfire Jasper has reduced lodging stock and potential course adjustments
Field of 300+ is small — limited competitive depth and social energy compared to major fondos
National park lodging is premium-priced and books months in advance
biased_opinion.bottom_line: The most beautiful road fondo in Canada, and a legitimate contender for the most scenic one-day cycling event in North America. You pay the logistics tax to ride through a cathedral.

final_verdict.one_liner: Canada's only national park fondo. 165 km through the Canadian Rockies. Marmot Basin — Alberta's #1 climb. The Icefields Parkway. Jasper. A cathedral on two wheels.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if riding through a UNESCO World Heritage national park in the Canadian Rockies is your idea of a perfect day on a bike. Yes if the Icefields Parkway, glaciers, and mountain wildlife matter more to you than competitive depth or field size. Skip if the 4-hour drive from Edmonton and limited Jasper lodging is a dealbreaker. Skip if you need a large, competitive field — this is 300 riders enjoying mountains, not 3,000 racing each other.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a bigger Canadian gran fondo: RBC GranFondo Whistler (3,000+ riders, Sea-to-Sky Highway). For more competitive Canadian climbing: Highwood Pass Gran Fondo. For the Icefields Parkway experience without a race: self-supported Jasper-to-Banff ride (290 km). For European national park riding: Gran Fondo Stelvio.

videos.title: 6 Etiquette Tips For Gran Fondos & Sportives
videos.description:
We've got 6 tips for you if you are taking part in a mass participation ride such as the Maratona dles Dolomites

Subscribe to GCN: http://gcn.eu/SubscribeToGCN
Get exclusive GCN gear in the GCN shop! http://gcn.eu/9O

It's important to stay safe and maintain good etiquette and style when riding and even more so in events. Ex-Professional Cyclists Dan Lloyd and Matt Stephens have some top tips so you can really enjoy your ride.

Let us know your tips in the comments 👇

Tips we mention are: 

- Don't move erratically or brake sharply. There is often a huge number of riders and you need to be aware of them to ensure safety for both yourself and others.
- Don't overlap the wheel in front. Overlapping the wheel in front of you can be dangerous and you cannot predict what the rider in front may do. Should they deviate from their line, your front wheel is at risk of being hit from the side by their back wheel.
- It's not a race. Many riders get carried away and get into a race mood/mentality. Obey the rules of the road and don't take risks.
- Warn others of upcoming hazards. Try to warn other riders of any hazards on the roads, such as potholes, parked cars, broken glass etc.
- Do you turn on the front. If you are in a group, there is no harm in taking advantage of sheltering from the wind, but make sure you also give a turn on the front if you can.
- Take care when snotting... If you need to empty your nostrils, ensure that you don't cover the rider(s) behind.


If you'd like to contribute captions and video info in your language, here's the link 👍  http://gcn.eu/9P

videos.title: What does it take to WIN a GranFondo!? - Les Trois Ballons 2022
videos.description:
How hard is it to Win a Granfondo? Or even just stay with the first group? In this race I threw all my usual pacing strategy out the window and went FULL GAS uphill with the first group to see if I'd be able to keep up. 
At the time of this race my estimated FTP was about 385w at 82kg. I can push a lot of power but I'm a big guy so definitely in a disadvantage on the steeper climbs. This Granfondo is called Les Trois Ballons. It's in the Vosges in France, an area also featured in the Tour de France. The race is one of the bigger granfondo races in Europe by the same organisation of ''La Marmotte'', a race I did in 2017&2017. Les 3 Ballons goes over 6 cilmbs adding up to 4000m+ of elevation in 178km, so it's a big one. With a group of friends we made it into a cycling weekend with the guys.

videos.title: The Gran Fondo Badlands
videos.description: The Gran Fondo Badlands is an amazingly challenging and beautiful ride through the hoodoos and valleys of Dinosaur Land, Drumheller, Alberta. The varying distances available to ride make it an event for all level of cyclists.

terrain_notes.text: Dubai has silky smooth roads where 28mm tires are wide enough and you can run higher pressure than usual due to the smooth surface conditions.
Dubai conditions are dry and sandy which can affect chain wax durability over long distances.

gear_mentions.text: 28mm tires are wide enough for Dubai's smooth roads - wider wouldn't be faster in those conditions.
Steel straight spokes are recommended over carbon spokes because they're easier to replace in local bike shops if damaged during travel or crashes.
Vittoria Corsa tires recommended over Corsa Pro for better durability, reliability and puncture resistance - a few watts savings won't make up for a 3-hour walk in the desert if tires fail.
Swap compact crankset (34/50) for 39/53 gearing to handle the high speeds expected in Dubai racing conditions.

race_day_tips.text: Prioritize reliability over performance when traveling - bring your most reliable bike setup rather than the highest-end components to avoid mechanical issues far from home.
Hot wax your chain before racing in dry, sandy conditions for better durability - can last up to 1000km without reapplication.

additional_quotes.text: Dubai actually has a whole network of about 200km of dedicated cycling path. We stay in town square right next to the start of the Aludra cycling track. Flexibility is key here.

rider_intel.search_text: Based on rider experiences in Dubai racing, the terrain features silky smooth roads where 28mm tires are sufficient and higher tire pressure can be used effectively. The dry and sandy conditions present unique challenges for equipment durability. Riders emphasize the importance of reliable gear over high-end components when traveling, recommending steel spokes for easier repairs and Vittoria Corsa tires for better puncture resistance over the Pro version. The fast, flat roads require larger gearing than compact cranksets, with 39/53 chainrings preferred over 34/50 for the high speeds. Dubai offers 200km of dedicated cycling paths with excellent logistics including convenient accommodation near cycling infrastructure. Chain maintenance is critical in the dry conditions, with hot wax lasting up to 1000km. The racing emphasizes reliability and durability over marginal performance gains due to the remote desert location and difficulty accessing bike shop services.
